critic_raw:
ASSESSOR 1:
The paper reports a drop in ICU transfers and attributes it to the sepsis model, but simultaneously introduced a nurse-driven sepsis bundle and evening staffing increase. These co-interventions are direct confounders for the outcome claim. The study lacks a contemporaneous control site (Hospital B excluded for data quality reasons). The causal claim is not supported. The model may have predictive validity, but the deployment outcome claim requires a stronger design. Verdict: mixed — predictive claim plausible, causal outcome claim unsupported.
defender_raw:
ASSESSOR 2:
The model shows clinician agreement with top-decile alerts and was deployed with a fixed threshold — these are real operational evidence. However, the concurrent protocol change (nurse-driven bundle, staffing increase) means the observed ICU transfer reduction cannot be attributed specifically to the model. Hospital B's exclusion removes the only available control. The paper conflates predictive validity with causal treatment effect. The appropriate position is: model has demonstrated predictive value; causal outcome contribution requires empirical test. Verdict: empirical_test_agreed.
adjudication_raw:
ASSESSOR 3:
Two assessors converge: the causal outcome claim is confounded by concurrent protocol changes, and no contemporaneous control exists. The model may have genuine predictive value.

SYNTHESIS:
All three assessors identify the concurrent protocol change (nurse-driven bundle) as the primary confound, the absence of a control hospital as the structural gap, and the conflation of predictive validity with causal deployment effect as the analytical error. The model's operational utility is plausible but unconfirmed.

ETD SPECIFICATION: The case involves a contested empirical claim — whether the model contributed to improved outcomes beyond what the nurse-driven bundle alone would produce.
- Measure: ICU transfer rates at Hospital A vs. Hospital B (or other non-deploying control) over the same launch window; within-period AUROC and calibration at Hospital A comparing pre- and post-launch periods
- Success criterion: Hospital B shows similar ICU transfer rate reduction without the model in the same window — confirming the bundle and staffing changes explain Hospital A's improvement
- Failure criterion: Hospital B shows no improvement in ICU transfers; Hospital A improvement is specifically concentrated in the subset of patients for whom model alerts were followed; within-period model calibration is maintained post-launch

empirical_test.measure: ICU transfer rates at Hospital A vs. Hospital B over the same period; within-period AUROC/calibration at Hospital A pre vs. post launch
empirical_test.success_criterion: Hospital B shows similar improvement without model — confirming bundle/secular trends explain Hospital A's outcome change
empirical_test.failure_criterion: Hospital B unchanged; Hospital A improvement concentrated in alert-followed patients; model maintains within-period calibration
